metadata.title: Strategic Infrastructure & Vulnerability Assessment: Iran Conflict Theater
metadata.purpose: Factual reference data for geopolitical simulation engine
metadata.disclaimer: Data compiled from open-source intelligence, government reports, and academic sources

iran_geography.description: Iran's geography makes ground invasion extraordinarily difficult — arguably impossible at scale in modern warfare

terrain_breakdown.notes: Roughly one-third rugged mountains (Zagros and Alborz ranges), over half arid desert (Dasht-e Kavir, Dasht-e Lut), remainder cultivated or urban

zagros_mountains.description: Natural fortress running 1,500 km from northwest to southeast Iran, forming the western defensive barrier
zagros_mountains.military_significance: Folded sedimentary rock layers create irregular terrain that impedes mechanized movement and complicates targeting. Historical invasion routes through Zagros passes are narrow, easily defended chokepoints.

alborz_mountains.description: Northern mountain range protecting Tehran from the Caspian littoral
alborz_mountains.military_significance: Shields Tehran from northern approach; extremely limited road access through passes

distances_border_to_tehran_km.notes: All distances approximate as-the-crow-flies. Road distances significantly longer due to mountain terrain. Baghdad to Tehran driving distance is 877 km across Zagros Mountains.

historical_invasion_context.summary: Iran has not been successfully conquered and occupied in modern history. Anglo-Soviet invasion of 1941 succeeded only because Reza Shah capitulated after 6 days — and that involved two great powers attacking simultaneously from north and south with Iran's military ordered to stand down.
key_precedents: 1941 Anglo-Soviet invasion: Required simultaneous north-south attack; Iran's military stood down by royal order
1980-1988 Iran-Iraq War: Iraq invaded with surprise and superior equipment; failed to advance beyond border regions despite 8 years of war
Alexander the Great (330 BC): Took years to subdue Iranian plateau even with Persian Empire already collapsing

population.notes: Iran has mandatory 18-month military service for males. Basij militia is a volunteer paramilitary force with estimated 1M+ who could be mobilized. Total wartime mobilization capacity estimated at 2M+.
population.source: Worldometer; CIA World Factbook; IISS Military Balance

fordow.location: Near Qom, 160 km south of Tehran
fordow.bunker_buster_vulnerability: US GBU-57 MOP (30,000 lb) may not penetrate; would require multiple sequential strikes on same point
fordow.notes: Built inside mountain in Zagros range. Geological folding creates irregular layers that impede shockwave transmission.
fordow.source: ISIS Reports; IAEA; JFeed geological analysis

natanz.notes: Original above-ground centrifuge halls were vulnerable (hit in 2026 strikes). New underground complex tunneled into Zagros mountains, approximately 1 mile south of original facility. Depth may exceed Fordow.

other_suspected_sites: Underground missile production facilities (multiple locations in Zagros)
IRGC underground naval bases along Persian Gulf coast

infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.road_network: Limited highway corridors through mountain passes; easily blocked or destroyed
infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.rail_network: Single-track rail lines with limited capacity; no high-speed rail to border regions
infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.bridges: Critical chokepoints — relatively few bridges across major rivers and gorges
infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.supply_line_vulnerability: Any force advancing from Iraq border faces 650+ km supply line through mountain terrain with constant ambush risk
infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.urban_warfare: Tehran metro area: 16M+ people. Isfahan, Shiraz, Tabriz each 2M+. Urban warfare would be catastrophic.
infrastructure_limitations_for_invading_.climate: Extreme heat in south (50°C+), extreme cold in mountains (-30°C winter), sandstorms in desert regions

overview.notes: Combined Hormuz + Red Sea corridor carries critical intercontinental traffic between Europe, Asia, and Africa. These two chokepoints carry a combined ~30% of global internet traffic.
overview.source: TeleGeography; Submarine Networks; Rest of World

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: FLAG (Fiber-optic Link Around the Globe)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: UK to Japan via Mediterranean, Egypt, Gulf

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: SEA-ME-WE 3 (South East Asia–Middle East–Western Europe 3)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: Germany to Australia/Japan via Mediterranean, Red Sea, Indian Ocean

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: SEA-ME-WE 4
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: France to Singapore via Mediterranean, Egypt, Gulf

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: SEA-ME-WE 5
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: France to Singapore via Mediterranean, Red Sea, Indian Ocean

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: SEA-ME-WE 6 / Al Khaleej Cable System
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: Singapore to France with Gulf Extension through Strait of Hormuz
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.status: Active — Gulf Extension runs through Strait of Hormuz

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: AAE-1 (Asia-Africa-Europe 1)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: Hong Kong to France via SE Asia, India, Gulf, Egypt

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: IMEWE (India–Middle East–Western Europe)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: India to France via Gulf, Egypt

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: EIG (Europe India Gateway)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: UK to India via Mediterranean, Egypt, Gulf

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: FALCON (FLAG Alcatel-Lucent Optical Network)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: India to Gulf states and Egypt

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: Gulf Bridge International (GBI)
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.status: Active — only major cable with Iranian landing station

major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.name: 2Africa
major_cables_through_hormuz_and_gulf.route: Circumnavigates Africa, connects Europe, Middle East, Asia

fujairah_uae.description: Primary Gulf cable landing hub, located on Gulf of Oman coast (outside Strait of Hormuz)
fujairah_uae.strategic_note: Most UAE cables land at Fujairah specifically to avoid routing through the Strait of Hormuz — but cables serving Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and Iraq must transit the Strait

iran_landing_stations.description: Iran has limited submarine cable connections
iran_landing_stations.notes: Iran's limited cable connectivity means it relies heavily on terrestrial links through Turkey and Turkmenistan

vulnerability_assessment.cable_depth_in_strait_m: 50-100 in shallow sections, up to 200+ in deeper areas
iranian_threats: Naval mines — Iran has estimated 5,000-6,000 mines of various types
Ghadir-class midget submarines — can operate in shallow waters near cables
IRGC naval special forces — capable of diver-placed charges
Anchoring/dragging — deliberate anchor damage in known cable routes
vulnerability_assessment.repair_time_estimate: 2-8 weeks per cable depending on damage location and availability of cable repair ships

economic_impact_if_severed.estimated_daily_cost_usd: Hundreds of millions to billions — difficult to quantify precisely
economic_impact_if_severed.affected_services: Cloud computing, financial transactions, streaming, enterprise communications
economic_impact_if_severed.notes: India alone routes significant Europe-bound traffic through Gulf cables. GCC data center industry ($5B+) depends on cable connectivity.

vulnerability_assessment.source: TeleGeography; Rest of World; Capacity Media

overview.description: GCC countries are among the most water-scarce nations on Earth. Most depend on energy-intensive desalination for the majority of their drinking water. Destruction of desalination plants would create a humanitarian catastrophe within days.

qatar.natural_freshwater_resources: Virtually none — no permanent rivers or lakes
qatar.vulnerability_notes: Qatar's consumption-to-natural-freshwater ratio is among the highest in the world — estimated at 2,000-17,000x depending on measurement. With only ~2 days of reserves, loss of desalination means a water crisis within 48 hours.

kuwait.vulnerability_notes: Kuwait's groundwater is largely non-potable brackish water. Drinking water depends almost entirely on desalination.

uae.water_security_strategy: UAE 2036 Water Security Strategy — target of 45 days strategic reserves

major_desalination_plants.name: Jebel Ali
major_desalination_plants.notes: One of the world's largest desalination plants

major_desalination_plants.name: Taweelah
major_desalination_plants.notes: World's largest RO desalination plant when commissioned

uae.vulnerability_notes: UAE is better prepared than most GCC states with 45-day strategic reserves. However, groundwater is being depleted and is largely non-renewable fossil water.

major_desalination_plants.name: Ras Al-Khair
major_desalination_plants.notes: One of the world's largest desalination plants. Also generates 2,400 MW electricity.

saudi_arabia.vulnerability_notes: Saudi Arabia has most desalination capacity of any country. Eastern Province plants (Ras Al-Khair, Jubail) face the Persian Gulf and are within range of Iranian missiles. Red Sea coast plants provide geographic diversification.

bahrain.vulnerability_notes: Bahrain is the most geographically exposed GCC state — only 200 km from Iranian coast across the Persian Gulf. No strategic depth.

oman.vulnerability_notes: Oman has more groundwater than other GCC states but aquifers are being depleted. Omani facilities face the Gulf of Oman, not the Persian Gulf — slightly less exposed to Iranian missiles.

desalination_cost_context.energy_cost: Desalination consumes 15-20% of GCC electricity generation
desalination_cost_context.notes: Desalination plants are large, fixed, coastal targets — impossible to hide or harden significantly. Replacement takes 3-5 years to build.

the_17000x_statistic.description: Some GCC microstates consume water at a rate 2,000 to 17,000 times their natural renewable freshwater resources. This is most applicable to Qatar and the UAE, which have negligible natural freshwater. The exact multiplier depends on measurement methodology but illustrates extreme dependence on desalination.
the_17000x_statistic.source: World Resources Institute; various academic analyses

saudi_arabia.domestic_production: Growing wheat production (~500,000 tonnes), poultry sector expanding, dairy self-sufficient
saudi_arabia.notes: SAGO (Saudi Grains Organization) maintains strategic grain reserves. Saudi has most food security capacity in GCC.

uae.domestic_production: Dates, some vegetables via hydroponics, limited poultry
uae.notes: UAE has invested heavily in food tech, vertical farming, and overseas farmland acquisitions (esp. in Africa, Central Asia)

kuwait.notes: Extremely dependent on Hormuz for food imports. Most exposed GCC state to food disruption.

qatar.domestic_production: Expanded domestic dairy (Baladna) after 2017 blockade. Some greenhouse vegetables.
qatar.notes: 2017 GCC blockade was a wake-up call — Qatar invested in domestic food production and Turkish/Omani supply alternatives. Still overwhelmingly import-dependent.

bahrain.notes: Most food-insecure GCC state. Relies on King Fahd Causeway to Saudi Arabia as alternative to maritime imports.

oman.domestic_production: More agriculture than other GCC states — dates, limes, vegetables, fishing industry
oman.notes: Oman has ports on Gulf of Oman and Arabian Sea, providing alternative import routes that bypass Hormuz

suez_canal.source: Suez Canal Authority; Reuters; Atlas Institute

bab_el_mandeb.description: 26 km wide strait connecting Red Sea to Gulf of Aden — southern chokepoint controlling Suez Canal access

houthi_capabilities.naval_mines: Capability demonstrated — have deployed mines in Red Sea

demonstrated_effectiveness.ships_attacked_2023_2025: Over 100 commercial vessels attacked or harassed
demonstrated_effectiveness.trade_diversion_pct: 65% of container traffic rerouted away from Red Sea at peak
demonstrated_effectiveness.insurance_premium_increase: 10-50x increase in war risk premiums for Red Sea transit

houthi_capabilities.source: Wikipedia Red Sea crisis; Al Jazeera; Maplecroft

hezbollah_naval_capabilities.demonstrated: 2006 — hit INS Hanit (Israeli corvette) with C-802 missile
hezbollah_naval_capabilities.threat_area: Eastern Mediterranean, primarily approaches to Israeli ports

cape_of_good_hope.notes: Rerouting around Africa adds ~10 days and ~$1M fuel per voyage for large container ships

east_west_pipeline_alternatives.petroline_saudi: East-West crude oil pipeline — 5M bpd capacity, bypasses Hormuz to Red Sea terminal at Yanbu
east_west_pipeline_alternatives.habshan_fujairah_uae: Abu Dhabi crude oil pipeline — 1.5M bpd, bypasses Hormuz to Fujairah on Gulf of Oman
east_west_pipeline_alternatives.notes: These pipelines can partially offset Hormuz closure for oil but not for LNG, containers, or food

insurance_impact.notes: War risk premiums for Hormuz transit have reached 1%+ of hull value during active hostilities — for a $150M vessel, that's $1.5M per transit

strait_of_hormuz_oil_flow.primary_destination: Asia (90% of Hormuz exports, with China and India receiving 44%)

key_facilities.name: Abqaiq
key_facilities.significance: World's largest oil processing facility. Processes majority of Saudi crude before export.
key_facilities.precedent: September 2019 drone/missile attack temporarily knocked out 5.7M bpd — over 5% of global supply
key_facilities.vulnerability: Demonstrated vulnerability to drone and cruise missile attack

key_facilities.name: Ras Tanura
key_facilities.significance: Saudi Arabia's largest offshore crude loading terminal + major refinery. Critical for meeting export commitments.
key_facilities.notes: Loading capacity handles millions of bpd of crude exports

key_facilities.name: Ghawar
key_facilities.significance: World's largest conventional oil field. Multiple processing facilities across 280 km.

key_facilities.name: Yanbu
key_facilities.significance: Alternative export route bypassing Hormuz. Connected to East-West pipeline.

key_facilities.name: North Field / Ras Laffan
key_facilities.significance: Single largest LNG production site in the world. Approximately one-quarter of all global LNG trade comes from this one location.
key_facilities.expansion_plan: North Field Expansion (NFE) to increase to 126 mtpa by 2027
key_facilities.vulnerability: Concentrated single point of failure. Within range of Iranian ballistic missiles (~250 km across Gulf). No geographic diversification.

north_field_gas_reserves.description: World's largest non-associated natural gas field, shared with Iran's South Pars
north_field_gas_reserves.notes: Iran and Qatar share this massive gas field — complicates targeting decisions

key_facilities.name: Ruwais
key_facilities.significance: One of the world's largest integrated refining and petrochemical complexes

key_facilities.name: Das Island
key_facilities.significance: Processes and exports crude oil, LNG, LPG, naphtha, and sulfur. Plans to increase LNG capacity by 0.9 mtpa.

key_facilities.name: Habshan-Fujairah Pipeline
key_facilities.route: Abu Dhabi to Fujairah (Gulf of Oman)
key_facilities.significance: Allows UAE to export crude bypassing Strait of Hormuz. Critical strategic asset.

key_facilities.name: Mina Al-Ahmadi
key_facilities.significance: Kuwait's oldest and most strategic refinery. Includes offshore loading terminals.

kuwait_knpc.vulnerability_notes: Kuwait's refineries are concentrated on the Persian Gulf coast, ~250 km from Iranian coast. No bypass pipeline exists — all exports must transit Hormuz.

key_facilities.name: Kharg Island
key_facilities.significance: Iran's primary oil export terminal — handles ~96% of Iran's oil exports. Destruction would eliminate Iran's oil export capability.
key_facilities.vulnerability: Island facility — exposed to air and naval attack. Was attacked repeatedly during Iran-Iraq War (1984-1988).

key_facilities.name: Asaluyeh / South Pars
key_facilities.significance: Iran's primary gas processing hub for the South Pars field (shared with Qatar's North Field). Processes ~600 mcm/day of natural gas.

key_facilities.name: Bandar Abbas Refinery
key_facilities.significance: Iran's largest refinery by capacity. Located directly on the Strait.

key_facilities.name: Isfahan Refinery
key_facilities.significance: Iran's highest-capacity refinery. Interior location provides some protection.

key_facilities.name: Abadan Refinery
key_facilities.significance: Historically one of the world's largest refineries. Heavily damaged in Iran-Iraq War, since rebuilt.

war_termination_scenarios.description: Realistic conditions under which each major actor would seek to end hostilities, based on historical precedent and structural constraints

domestic_pressure_triggers: US military casualties exceeding 500 KIA — public tolerance threshold based on post-9/11 wars
Oil prices sustained above $150/bbl causing recession and voter backlash
Congressional opposition / War Powers Act challenges gaining momentum
2026 midterm elections (November) — if war becomes unpopular, electoral pressure mounts
Allied GCC civilian casualties from Iranian retaliation creating diplomatic strain
political_termination.coalition_collapse_risk: Low — Israel is fully committed. GCC states may privately seek de-escalation but won't publicly break with US.

economic_termination.notes: Trump administration particularly sensitive to stock market and gas prices. A recession would be politically devastating.

military_termination.carrier_vulnerability_threshold: Loss of a major surface combatant (destroyer or above) would be politically shattering
escalation_red_lines: Nuclear weapon use by any party
Major humanitarian catastrophe attributed to US strikes

face_saving_off_ramps: Declare nuclear sites 'destroyed' and pivot to diplomatic phase — 'mission accomplished' framing
Announce bilateral negotiations with Iran on 'comprehensive deal'
UN Security Council resolution as face-saving framework
Point to regime behavior change / concessions as victory
Ceasefire tied to IAEA inspections regime

domestic_pressure_triggers: Hezbollah rocket campaign making northern Israel uninhabitable for extended period
Reservist fatigue — Israel's economy depends on reservists returning to civilian jobs
US withdrawing support or pressuring ceasefire
political_termination.coalition_stability: Netanyahu government depends on war for political survival — war termination creates domestic political risk for leadership

economic_termination.credit_downgrade_risk: Extended war leads to further sovereign rating downgrades
economic_termination.notes: Israel's economy contracted significantly during 2023-2024 Gaza operations. Extended multi-front war is unsustainable beyond months.

military_termination.iron_dome_interceptor_depletion: Iron Dome / David's Sling interceptor stocks are finite — production cannot match sustained high-volume attacks
military_termination.air_force_attrition: Any loss of F-35s is strategically significant given small fleet size
military_termination.multi_front_strain: Simultaneous Gaza, Lebanon, and Iran operations stretch IDF beyond capacity for sustained operations

face_saving_off_ramps: Declare Iran nuclear program 'set back 10+ years'
Negotiate Hezbollah withdrawal from southern Lebanon as strategic gain
International security guarantees for Israel as part of diplomatic framework
Demilitarized zones and monitoring regimes as tangible outcomes

domestic_pressure_triggers: Regime survival threat — if war threatens IRGC/clerical hold on power
Popular uprising risk — economic devastation could trigger domestic unrest (2019/2022 protest precedent)
Ethnic separatism risk — war stress could activate Kurdish, Baloch, Arab separatist movements
Supreme Leader succession crisis (post-Khamenei assassination in scenario)
political_termination.regime_calculus: Regime survival is the supreme objective — Iran will accept significant military losses before accepting terms that threaten regime continuity

economic_termination.hyperinflation_threshold: Already under severe sanctions — wartime economy can sustain for months on autarky model
economic_termination.notes: Iran has experience operating under maximum pressure sanctions since 2018. Economic pain tolerance is high but not infinite.

military_termination.proxy_force_decapitation: Loss of Hezbollah leadership + supply lines significantly reduces Iran's regional leverage
military_termination.notes: Iran cannot win a conventional war against US/Israel. Strategy is to inflict enough pain to force negotiated outcome.

face_saving_off_ramps: Sanctions relief package presented as diplomatic victory
Framing survival itself as victory — 'resistance won' narrative (Hezbollah 2006 model)
Nuclear deal with enhanced status (recognized as threshold state)
Reconstruction aid and diplomatic recognition package
Regional security framework that gives Iran a formal seat at the table

domestic_pressure_triggers: Civilian casualties from Iranian missile/drone attacks
Water crisis if desalination plants hit — 48 hours to catastrophe
Economic devastation from Hormuz closure — oil can't export, food can't import
Expatriate exodus — GCC economies depend on foreign workers who would flee
Popular unrest risk (especially Bahrain's Shia majority population)
political_termination.coalition_dynamics: GCC states are reluctant participants — none want to be on the front line. Would push hard for US to negotiate ceasefire.

oil_revenue_loss_per_day_usd_million.notes: Combined GCC oil/gas revenue loss exceeds $1.4B/day during Hormuz closure

economic_termination.notes: GCC sovereign wealth funds provide enormous financial buffer, but cannot substitute for water and food security. Economic pain is secondary to physical survival.

military_termination.gcc_military_role: Primarily defensive — providing basing, air defense, logistical support to US forces
military_termination.notes: GCC states are not primary combatants but bear disproportionate risk from Iranian retaliation

face_saving_off_ramps: Regional security framework including Iran — reduces future threat
Quiet bilateral engagement with Iran — most GCC states have back-channel relationships
Present post-war reconstruction as regional leadership opportunity
Energy market stabilization and price recovery as economic justification
